speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number six. July 12, 1995. Izchak Goldblatt. Why did you want to make this tape today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, two reasons why I wanted to make this tape-- one is because I heard and I see that people are starting to deny that there was a-- there was a holocaust while we are still some of us are still alive. Now, what will happen when we are all dying? And we are close-- close by, close to dying. I mean, we are dying out. It's a-- it's a-- it's a dying out species, the Auschwitz survivors. What will happen then?

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wanted to leave a testimony that nobody can deny that I was personally there, and I went through this, and I have only told a very small part because I cannot even tell. I cannot remember and cannot recall everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another reason is because I am afraid of another Holocaust in the United States or-- or some other place, probably in the United States, because there are a-- there are a lot of Jewish people here. And I will tell you why. You will have to bear with me. I have to tell you a personal little story, and then you will understand why. One day, about three years ago or four years ago, I went to make a cataract operation on my-- on my left eye. And after a year, I got a secondary-- what is called a secondary cataract. Only about 2% of cataract operated people get a secondary cataract. I was one of the lucky ones who got a secondary cataract.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I called my doctor. He said, yeah. It's a big laser. It's easily done. It cost $2,000. I said, $2,000? I can't afford to pay that money. That was when I already had-- didn't have much money left from what I came with. So I went to my neighbor, and her son-in-law called another doctor, a colleague of his who has this laser machine, and he agreed to make it for-- make the operation for $600. I went to the doctor. And he tells me, put your chin up here. And I think he's checking my eye. And I hear five clicks-- tick, tick, tick, tick, tick. And he says, 30 seconds, we are done. I could have made it in 27, but what is the rush? And I was-- I was-- I was so shocked. He operated on me with a laser machine. He made-- he drilled five holes in my lens. And he said, in about an hour and a half, you will see perfectly well like you see-- like you saw before. So I go home, sit down on my bed, and put the television on. And I'm watching to see when I will get clear vision with my-- in my left eye. And then I saw-- I see a picture on the screen where-- from the-- of a person, the head of a person where from his nose, nostrils, from his eyes, and from his ears, come streams of blood. Was this Halloween time?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Halloween time. And the-- and the speaker says, and now, we will give you 10 hours of horror films. And I say to myself, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], now 10 hours of this-- this is what they are feeding our children? Because during the day, I never see television, only in the nighttime. Here, I see this is what they are giving. Probably children are watching this. This was like 5:00 in the afternoon, 5:30 in the afternoon. So while I was sitting on the bed, I say to myself, society has gone crazy. If this is what they will feed our kids for 10 hours, then society is getting crazy. And then came-- it came to me that in the Bible, we already have societies that had-- have gone crazy. And I remembered what happened. The Bible tells us about the Babylonians who started building a tower. They had this technology of building, so they wanted to build a tower that will go up to the sky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they will fight God. They will throw God down from there. And they will rule everything. So what can you call that? Isn't that the craziness? Why you going to fight God? That-- that's-- I call that the craziness of that society.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw another thing that was-- has worried me so much. The Midrash tells us that when they were building the tower, they created a kind of human elevator. People were going constantly up and down, up and down, bringing the materials, and bringing from up orders what they need. If somebody came up with the wrong material, they would hit it-- him in the head, and he would fall down on the ground. It would kill him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I saw-- I saw that where human life was not-- not considered for-- worth anything, and only technology was considered. That if a society is advancing technologically too much, and biologically and morally is standing back, that society must get crazy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And just thinking, without going into deep research, I remembered what happened to Pharaoh. Egypt was the most advanced, most technologically advanced and-- and-- and-- and intelligent people were living there. They were building-- they were building the pyramids that we don't know to this day how they did it. They are-- they were balming corpses that stay-- that stay--

speaker: interviewer
text: Embalming, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: To this day. And we don't know-- still don't know how they did it. They were such-- so-- such advanced people. And the ruler of those people, the Pharaoh, the Midrash tells us the following story about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day, he got a skin rash. And his doctors told him that if you go away, if he will bathe in kids' blood-- young kids' blood. So every morning, they would slaughter 150 Jewish kids, and the Pharaoh would bathe in their blood. Now, who in a sane person, who would go and bathe in blood of other human beings? And another story the Bible tells us, that the Jewish people were-- were-- were condemned to work, and they had to make a certain amount of bricks every day. If one of them was missing a break, they would take one of his children, put it into the wall instead of a brick. Now, who sane people would do something like that? So I came again to the conclusion that the society got crazy because all they were doing was trying to advance technologically, building-wise, balming, doing things like those. But morally, human beings, human life was not considered worth anything. Later, I saw what happened to me in Auschwitz. I thought about Hitler. When we spoke in our house about Germany or about something German, it was with the greatest of respects because the Germans were the most cultured people in Europe and maybe in the whole world. They love music. They love opera. They love poetry. They love paintings. They love women. They love children. They love horses. They love dogs. They love flowers. They love everything beautiful that God created. Now, how can you believe that a society like that, a person can take a child, a baby, throw it in the air and four other to shoot at it to see who can-- to make a game of it-- who can shoot more-- more times to that baby? Isn't that the craziness of society?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Germany did put the accentuation only on building, only on technology, only-- only on weaponry. Human life was not considered worth anything. So I come to the conclusion that whenever a society is advancing technologically so much and morally so little, that society has to get crazy, because history is repeating itself. I know of other cases from our Bible, from our Talmud where it happened, but I'm not going into it now. So this is why I am so scared and so afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day at the dinner-- we were at the dinner at the Hilton Hotel. Somebody introduced me to a certain priest, an evangelist, who speaks on the radio. And he told to me-- told me that this man, you see this man, he has 40 million listeners. I said, you know something? I have something to tell to this man. And he said, OK, I'll introduce you. We happened to sit at the same table. He introduces me. And after that man gave his speech, I say to him, Mr. Ed McAteer, would you-- could you spare five minutes of your time for a simple person like me? He said, yes. When would you like those five minutes? I said, how about now? He said, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Other people went to dance. We sat in a corner. And I told him the story of my operation with the eye. And I say to him, listen, on one hand, look what America is doing-- operations without even my knowledge, without a knife, without blood, without anesthesia. And on the other hand, they go-- they go to the moon, and now, they are going further than the moon. And look, they are killing people on the subway. Look how much hatred there is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why can't they see that they have to take the monies from the-- from those technological researchers and put it into education? They have to educate the people that human life is the most important thing in the world. And-- and I said, I will not keep you more than five minutes. And I didn't keep him more than five minutes. The man was sitting open-mouthed. And he said to me-- after I finished, he said, you know something? We all know this, but we never think about it. But you are right. You know, you are right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We will have a convention on the 20th of August. We will have a convention in Dallas, Texas. There will be four ex-presidents. There will be the Vice President of now, and there will be dignitaries, and I give you five minutes on the microphone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to him, and who am I going to say I am, Izchak [? Bensura? ?] I am Isaac, the son of Sarah, who was on the altar, who was in Auschwitz, who was-- who was on the altar. And I didn't have the-- the-- the lamb instead of me. What am I going? I am going to tell the leaders of today, I'm going to tell them how to educate the people? Who are-- are they going to listen to me? No, I want you to speak. So he said, you know what? Type it down. Write it down for me, and I will-- I will say it at the convention. Now, I-- I typed it down. I worked hard to type it down as short as I could, and I gave it to him-- to my friend. And I don't know. Did he say it? Did he read it for anybody? I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just pray. And this is the other reason-- because I am scared of the hatred when I see so much technology here and so little care about human life that-- that they are killing in-- in the subway for a leather jacket, they are killing a man. And for a portable radio, they are killing another man. And for small things, human life became so cheap. And the hatred is so bitter that I am afraid another Holocaust is coming. This society is also getting crazy. So I say, help. I'm crying for help. Anybody who has a say, anybody who has a word with a microphone, anybody who speaks to people, anybody who writes in a newspaper or writes books, scream loud. Help. Because otherwise, this is going to be the end of everything else. This society is also getting crazy. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] Izchak, can you introduce this lovely woman who has joined you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. This is my wife for the last 33 years and something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's-- what's your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miriam.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is there something you'd like to say about your husband doing this tape today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to say that I think that it's very important for him to let the world know not to forget what happened to him and for all the Jewish people that were in the lagers. And I hope that he will take from now on easier, because he shared with everybody his feelings. Maybe he gave up some burden that he had on his shoulder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry, my son is out. He had a Hatzolah call. So saving a life is more important than taking pictures. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This is a picture of my sister Hannah. She is the only-- youngest child in-- in our family. And when we were liberated, I was so sorry that I don't have somebody to care for because I thought now, after the war, I will be able to care about-- about the whole world. That's how we felt after liberation immediately. But then, we knew better. So this picture of was taken on the 18th of July, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that during the war, you were dreaming of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, when I was liberated, I was dreaming more of her than about my parents because I so much wanted her to be-- to be alive so that I can take care of her. But it wasn't meant to be. She never came home. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my oldest brother, David, who-- and his wife, and my-- one of my sisters called Nellie. His wife, Rosa, passed away in Israel after the war. Nellie is living in Canada today, in Windsor, Canada. And David was laboring, making trenches for the-- for the Hungarian army when they were captured by the Russians and taken into a train. And the typhoid broke out, so the Russians just torched the whole train and burned everybody alive. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture again of my brother, David, who I spoke about earlier and my older sister Regina, who lives now in Canada. And she has a son living in Kingston, Canada. And we have seen her recently. Last three weeks ago, I have been there to visit them. She's about 80 years old, but she's well, thank God. And I hope she stays well. The picture of was taken somewhere before the war. I don't remember the exact date. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of my Uncle Isaac. He died at the age of 73 in Sighet. He wasn't deported. He used to come every Friday night. And we would march in the kitchen up and down like soldiers. And he loved his sister, my mother, so much that he would-- every occasion he would have, he would come in. And-- and he loved us, my mother's children, all of them, and we loved him dearly. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was made on 18th of July, 1944. From right to left-- my sister Regina, who lives in Canada, my youngest sister Hanni, who didn't come back, myself, my sister Esther, who lives in Tel Aviv, my sister Nellie, who lives in Windsor, Canada, and in the center, my mother-- aleha ha-shalom-- who didn't come back from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was '43 or '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was '43-- 18th of July, '43. I'm mis-saying '44 all the time. And my father was in the window when he took this picture. And he just didn't want to come and have-- take a picture with us I don't know why. And we don't have one picture from my father, unfortunately. So this is all there is left. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture has been taken immediately after the war in 1946. I am all blown up. I've never been so thick in my life. This is, myself-- from right to left, myself, my brother, Sigmund, in the middle, and my brother Jacob. They both live in Israel, and I will join them soon in Israel, in Tel Aviv. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS] This is a picture taken about three, four years ago-- that would be like 1992-- taken here in the synagogue of our Young Israel of Jamaica Estates-- myself, my wife, Miriam, and our son, Gili, who is with us now. But he is working for Hatzolah. And day and night, he is running around saving people's lives. [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS] This picture has been taken-- taken about four years ago in Israel. And the occasion was the bat mitzvah of my granddaughter, Roni, who is there on the picture with her sister, Tal. That's Miriam, my wife, and myself. [PAUSES FOR 28 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is our newest grandson, Ori. He was born in Tel Aviv in 1993, in April, somewhere in 1993. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 8th? 8th of April, 1993. [LAUGHS] She knows those dates.
